title_es: ¡Por qué a EE.UU. le encantan los colores amarillo 5 y 6!
hook_en: She bites a candy and gasps, 'Did you know?'
hook_es: Ella muerde un dulce y exclama, '¿Sabías?'
key_message: Yellow 5 and 6 are banned in several countries for causing hyperactivity in kids, but they're still in US foods.
visual_elements: Quick cut to a map highlighting Norway, Finland, and Austria
Overlaid warning labels used in the EU
Split screen showing candy with and without additives
_source_regulation_context:
ADDITIVES BANNED IN OTHER COUNTRIES BUT LEGAL IN US:
  • Yellow 5 (Tartrazine) & Yellow 6 (Sunset Yellow): Candy, cereals, sodas, sports drinks, mac & cheese — Banned in: Norway, Finland (restricted), Austria — Health concern: EU requires warning labels — 'may have adverse effect on activity and attention in children'

US OBESITY RATES OVER TIME:
  • 1960: 13.4% (adults), 5.0% (children)
  • 1980: 15.0% (adults), 5.5% (children)
  • 1994: 22.5% (adults), 10.0% (children)
  • 2000: 30.5% (adults), 13.9% (children)
  • 2010: 35.7% (adults), 16.9% (children)
  • 2016: 39.8% (adults), 18.5% (children)
  • 2020: 41.9% (adults), 19.7% (children)
  • 2024: 43.0% (adults), 21.0% (children)

title_en: US Obesity: What's Really Fueling the Epidemic?
title_es: Obesidad en EE.UU.: ¿Qué está alimentando la epidemia?
hook_en: She holds a fast-food bag, 'Guess what changed since 1960?'
hook_es: Ella sostiene una bolsa de comida rápida, '¿Adivina qué cambió desde 1960?'
key_message: Deregulations have led to an explosion in obesity rates, linking ultra-processed foods to rising health issues.
visual_elements: Timeline chart showing rising obesity rates
Fast motion video of her throwing processed food items into a bin
Before and after shot with a 1960s dinner and a modern fast food meal
